title: Pediatric Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis: Pain, Hydration, and Comfort
subtitle: An 8-year-old boy with sickle cell disease (HbSS) arrives with severe pain in his back and legs after a cold swim and poor fluid intake. The nurse manages the crisis with around-the-clock analgesia, hydration, oxygen, and warmth — and watches for life-threatening complications.

patient.name: Marcus Bell
patient.admitDate: Pediatric unit for an acute vaso-occlusive pain crisis. Known HbSS sickle cell disease. Severe pain (9/10) in back and legs after a cold pool and two days of poor intake; low-grade fever. On scheduled IV opioids, IV fluids, and oxygen; penicillin prophylaxis at home.

vitals.temp: Severe pain 9/10 back/legs after cold swim
Dry mucous membranes; poor intake x2 days

nursesNote: Marcus Bell, 8, HbSS sickle cell disease in an acute vaso-occlusive crisis: severe back/leg pain (9/10) after a cold swim and poor intake, low-grade fever, SpO2 94%, Hgb 7.8. On scheduled IV opioids, IV fluids, and oxygen. Care = prompt around-the-clock analgesia, hydration, oxygen, and WARMTH (never cold). Monitor closely for acute chest syndrome and splenic sequestration; reinforce trigger avoidance and the fever-emergency rule.
providerOrders: Around-the-clock IV opioid analgesia with a pediatric pain scale; reassess often
IV isotonic fluids; encourage oral intake
Oxygen to keep SpO2 within target
Warm compresses, rest, treat infection; continue penicillin prophylaxis
Monitor for acute chest syndrome and splenic sequestration; daily hemoglobin

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is happening in a vaso-occlusive crisis?

opts.text: Sickled cells block small vessels → tissue ischemia and severe pain
Common triggers include dehydration, infection, cold, hypoxia, and stress
Severe pain in the back, legs, and abdomen is typical
Fever can signal infection — a serious risk in sickle cell disease
Crisis pain is usually mild and needs no opioids
These children have functional asplenia and high infection risk
Treat pain promptly with scheduled (around-the-clock) analgesia, often opioids
Hydration (IV and oral) helps reduce sickling
Give oxygen if the child is hypoxic
Use WARMTH (warm compresses) — never cold, which worsens vasoconstriction
Fluids should be restricted during a crisis
Watch for acute chest syndrome and splenic sequestration — both emergencies

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the management of the crisis

opts.text: Assess pain, airway/breathing, and oxygenation
Start prompt around-the-clock analgesia for severe pain
Provide IV/oral hydration and oxygen as needed
Apply warmth, promote rest, and treat any infection
Monitor for complications (acute chest, sequestration) and document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Indicated or contraindicated in a crisis?

opts.text: Around-the-clock opioids guided by a pain scale
Generous IV and oral fluids
Supplemental oxygen for hypoxia
Warm compresses to painful areas
Encourage rest and treat infection promptly
Cold/ice packs to painful joints
Restricting fluids to "rest the gut"
Withholding opioids over addiction fears in a true crisis
Using meperidine (Demerol) as the routine analgesic

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess pain and respiratory status with the right tools
Give the ordered around-the-clock analgesia and reassess effect
Begin IV/oral hydration and oxygen as needed
Apply warmth, position for comfort, and promote rest
Monitor for acute chest/sequestration and teach trigger avoidance
Pain score drops and the child rests comfortably
Hydration restored; mucous membranes moist with good urine output
SpO2 stays at/above 95% with no chest symptoms
Parents can name triggers and the fever-emergency rule
New chest pain and fever with a falling SpO2 and infiltrate
Rapid spleen enlargement with falling hemoglobin and shock
Pain stays severe because doses are skipped or delayed
Cold packs are applied and the pain intensifies

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
